questions.q: What is the primary goal of the rasterization algorithm?
options: Convert geometric primitives into pixel fragments for rendering
Apply perspective transformations to vertices
Calculate lighting for each vertex
Compress geometry data for storage
questions.explanation: Rasterization converts continuous vector primitives (triangles) into discrete pixel fragments. These fragments then proceed through per-fragment operations like depth testing and blending.

questions.q: In scanline rasterization, what does the algorithm process?
options: Individual pixels in no particular order
Entire horizontal lines of pixels from top to bottom
Vertical columns of pixels from left to right
Pixels in a spiral pattern from center outward
questions.explanation: Scanline rasterization processes the screen from top to bottom, one horizontal line at a time. It determines which pixels fall inside triangles for each scanline efficiently.

questions.q: What is the purpose of anti-aliasing in rasterization?
options: Reduce the size of on-screen triangles
Remove jagged edges caused by the discrete pixel grid
Improve vertex shading quality
Increase the framerate
questions.explanation: Anti-aliasing reduces aliasing artifacts (jagged, stair-stepped edges) that occur when mapping continuous geometry to a discrete pixel grid. Techniques like MSAA sample multiple positions per pixel.

questions.q: In triangle rasterization, how is it determined if a pixel center is inside a triangle?
options: Using Euclidean distance to vertices
Using barycentric coordinates or edge functions
By comparing depth values
Through texture coordinate lookup
questions.explanation: Edge functions or barycentric coordinates test if a pixel is inside a triangle. Barycentric coordinates also enable smooth interpolation of vertex attributes like colors and normals.

questions.q: What does multisample anti-aliasing (MSAA) do differently than single-sample rasterization?
options: Renders the scene at higher resolution then downsamples
Samples coverage at multiple positions within each pixel
Applies Gaussian blur to edges
Reduces the number of pixels evaluated
questions.explanation: MSAA evaluates pixel coverage at multiple sample points within each pixel, not just the center. This provides better edge quality than single sampling, trading pixel coverage tests for smoother antialiasing.

questions.q: In depth testing during rasterization, when is a fragment discarded?
options: When its depth value is greater than the stored z-buffer value (assuming standard setup)
When its color is the same as the background
When it lies outside the screen bounds
When its normal vector points away from the camera
questions.explanation: The z-buffer compares fragment depth to the stored value. If the fragment is farther (greater depth in standard conventions), it's discarded because closer geometry is already rendered.

questions.q: What information is interpolated across a rasterized triangle for the fragment shader?
options: Only the xyz coordinates of the triangle vertices
Vertex attributes like color, normal, and texture coordinates
Only lighting and shadow information
The viewing angle from each vertex
questions.explanation: Rasterization linearly interpolates all per-vertex attributes (colors, normals, texture coords, positions) across the triangle using barycentric coordinates or edges functions.

questions.q: How does hierarchical rasterization improve performance compared to naive scanline rasterization?
options: It sorts triangles by depth before rasterizing
It uses a coarse-to-fine approach, quickly rejecting large non-overlapping regions
It caches pixel colors from previous frames
It disables anti-aliasing to reduce computation
questions.explanation: Hierarchical rasterization uses a quadtree or similar structure to quickly reject large screen regions that don't overlap the triangle, reducing unnecessary pixel tests.

questions.q: In the context of rasterization, what does the term 'fragment' represent?
options: A vertex of a triangle
A partially processed pixel with interpolated attributes, before per-fragment operations
A texture color value
A memory block in the frame buffer
questions.explanation: A fragment is a candidate pixel generated by rasterization with interpolated vertex attributes (position, color, texture coord). It progresses through depth test, blending, and other per-fragment ops before becoming a final pixel.

questions.q: Why must texture coordinates be divided by the w-component during rasterization (perspective correction)?
options: To match the resolution of the texture
To account for perspective foreshortening and ensure correct texture mapping
To reduce texture aliasing
To speed up texture lookups
questions.explanation: Perspective-correct texture mapping divides by w to account for how texture density changes with distance. Without perspective correction, textures appear distorted on polygons receding into the distance.
